title: 2025/11/20 - PP (Part 1)
description: Thursday 20 November 2025| Primary Production Committee Hearing Evidence: Briefing on Government support for research and development in the primary industries Horticulture New Zealand Alexander Bisson, Risk Policy Advisor Dr Anna Rathe Ministry for Primary Industries Stuart Anderson, DDG Biosecurity Lisa Minthrop, Director Import & Export Standards Fleur Francois, Director, Diagnostics Readiness & Surveillance
descriptionSummary: Hearing Evidence. Briefing on Government support for research and development in the primary industries. Horticulture New Zealand

chunks.text: Contain IES and stuff. You
Alex Hi. Hi, London. And you first. So do you have anybody? Yes, it's morning,
one member of mine. So

big morning Everyone. Welcome to the Primary Production Select committee. Um, we've got you for 15 minutes, so we're in your hands, Right? Good morning. Good morning everyone. And thank you very much for the opportunity to speak to the Select Committee for primary production. Uh, my name is a Dr. Alexander Bison.

Um, and this is my colleague Dr. Um Anna, both from Horticulture New Zealand. Uh, horticulture New Zealand is a levy funded, uh, advocacy organization for New Zealand's, um, commercial fruit and vegetable growers representing, uh, more than 4,300 growers, um, who produce around a hundred different crops, both, um, supplying the domestic and the export markets.

And, uh, together with the government, um, the horticulture sector aims to double farm gate value by 2035. And if we are to achieve this goal, there is a need to protect the existing horticulture plantings that we already have, uh, as well as innovating the growth of the, of the sector. And for this, we have, uh, five points to discuss, uh, today in this matter.

Uh, the first one is, um, a strong biosecurity systems critical to protect New Zealand's existing horticulture. Sector horticulture contributes nearly to $5 billion in export revenue and to New Zealand GDP, and it's essential to domestic food supply. Uh, it is critical that the existing sector is protected from new and high impact organisms that could reduce yield, impact the quality of our produce, um, and jeopardize our current market access.

Also critical is access to international plant germ blossom. Uh, new germ blossom allows New Zealand crows, uh, to provide new offerings to the export and domestic market, and helps us to adapt to challenges to climate change and pest and disease. So striking the balance between Crow and protect, um, across the plant import system is, um, essential for long-term success of our sector.

And ultimately, um, pest and disease prevention is, um, is significantly cheaper than, um, than eradication management, especially to a sector in itself. Our second point is that, um, stakeholders from the entire supply chain must be included in plant import systems, discussions, plant importers and growers, all part of the same, uh, same, same supply chain.

And if the import systems fails to prevent, uh, new pesting diseases to arrive in New Zealand, growers will bear the risks and the costs of biosecurity conversion, um, uh, responses. If the system is unworkable for importers, crawls will miss out on access to new varieties and the innovation.

Our thirdly, um, Horton De Zealand supports an end to end review of the plant input system. Uh, MPI has initiated a plant input systems reform project, and with, for New Zealand being a strong and active collaborator on that project, uh, we believe this review is necessary as the current system is slow, costly, and, and, uh, stifles innovation in this case.

Uh, for maximum benefit, the review should take a wide scope, and we expect that the systems improvements that arise as a result of that review, um, will translate to cost and time savings over the whole import system. Our fourth point here is that MPIs post entry quarantine service costs, uh, could be structured to improve accessibility to the diversity of the sector.

Uh, h New Zealand believes the import systems cost structure is worth revisiting to improve affordability, um, for the industry. While maintaining high biosecurity standards, uh, significant fee increases may make importation to undesirable, especially for small and promising sectors. A review could include exploration of, uh, incremental fee increase, uh, allowing industries to adopt and plan ahead over time.

Um, the level of public benefit associated with new germ lessons could be revisited, um, and therefore the public private cost share, uh, and a long, uh, a low interest government loan for importers payable over an extended period. As we do understand that the upfront costs to importers take years to recuperate. And our final point is that, uh, HOR New Zealand supports the building of the plant health environment capability facilities up in Auckland.

That new facility will replace the current PHEL facility in East, which has long been identified as out of date, and no longer fit for purpose. We support the new facility being built in a way that ensures it is fit for purpose for the future. It is important to note that that facility has two principle functions, um, one, to provide biosecurity system services such as surveillance, diagnostics, export assurance, and managing, um, biosecurity, incursions and responses.

And the second is the plant input systems services, uh, such as post entry quarantine and diagnostic testing to separate things. Um, Horton believes that, um, it is important to decouple the building of the PHEC facility. From the review of the input system. Both can, uh, occur concurrently and both will benefit the sector.

Uh, thank you and happily. Um, welcome. Any questions, Steve? Brilliant. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, has the methodology and technology changed significantly in the last five years for, for, for testing? Germ plasm? Uh, the technologies are always evolving. Mm-hmm.

Uh, and we would expect that NPI is keeping an eye on those and will incorporate those as appropriate. Um, over time, I mean, the last five years, yes, things are always changing and they will continue to change into the future, and we would expect that to be, um, taken on board as appropriate in New Zealand.

So, is there a risk that we're overspending on something, that there's a cheaper option for now because of the technological innovation? We don't see that, uh, spending on the facility now means that we can't adopt the new innovation in the future. Um, we would expect the facility to be designed in a way that, um, we could incorporate new technologies as available.

Uh, and as Alex noted, it's, it's important to say that the facility has dual purposes. So one is the biosecurity biosecurity system services that we rely on every day, and the other part is the import, the germs import system services, which are also critical, but they're one part of the facility, not the entire facility.

So why would you do an end-to-end review of the current system but exclude one of the most significant parts of it? Uh, we didn't suggest that it would be excluded. Post entry quarantine is, uh, one part of the system, but an end-to-end review would look across, um, offshore facilities and Port Health standard development, et cetera, noting that post entry quarantine is one part of the entire system.

So it wouldn't be excluded. We would expect post entry quarantine is a, a part of the system that needs to exist, uh, in some shape or form onshore. Mm. So, so you could, in terms of what you talked about, the significant fee increases that, um, post entry, uh, quarantining is a potential barrier to the entry of new germ plasm.

Um, so you, and you mentioned public private partnerships, you are open to there being more than one import facility and quarantine facility in the country. And that that could be run by other such as out of Lincoln University. We don't have a view on that. You don't have a view? We have, we, we need a post quarantine facility that, or facilities that meets the required standards.

Final question, Mr. Chief? Yes. Um, so it's correct that the normal regulated germ plasm pathway is the safest pathway from a biosecurity point of view. And most of the issues we get, such as with Mum Raiders stink bag or hornets most recently is through the cargo e-commerce and freight That we need to be managing all pathways.

Yep. Uh, plant germplasm is a critically important pathway to be managing because those plants are then gonna get propagated up, um, sent around the country to be then growing on. So, But it's in the last three years, it's not the major problem for us in terms of biosecurity. It is not, it's got a robust system in place. It's potential to be That It, it, it is, it is working because we have a really good system in base account captures it.

We probably wanna wanna see it as the, the life plan imports is the highest risk pathway and therefore why the highest level of, um, management. Yes. But you do think that private partner, like for example, you think Lincoln Lincoln University could maintain the same high standard? We, I mean, when we said, we said the public private cost here, not a public-private partnership Okay.

Was the point that was raised about the fee structure. I guess one of the suggestions has been that there could be, um, more than one facility in the country and the technology as such that there are much lower cost ways of doing the same tests. It could be done in multiple locations.

There's, there's one additional risk with multiple locations for testing, which is that, um, some of the pathogens and pests that we're worried about are easily dispersed. And so moving them between facilities, uh, does create an additional risk. One final. 'cause currently you've only got one facility, so you now gotta move them from the north island to the south island, for example.

Wouldn't it be better if you wanted to report to the south island? You go to a facility there and you wanna report to the north island, you go to a facility there or a couple there, doesn't it reduce the amount of movement? The Material doesn't leave the facility until it's, um, being tested and is deemed high quality test free material. So what's The disadvantage in having one in or more than one in each island?

So a, a again, we do not oppose, um, uh, two, two facilities being, being, uh, in place. Yeah. It's not the point. Um, it might be important to see that the, the, the larger component of the cost for the facility for importers is actually the biocon containment component. The rent of the rooms to maintain, um, a safe environment Yeah.

To test that. Not necessarily the tests, because the tests can be run, uh, in other facilities on, on a different management. In, in, in the same way it's a biocon containment component that is critical. And as far as I understand, there is no other facility that can provide that. Yeah. Or it's planning to. Yeah. Okay. We have a question online from Mark den. You there, mark?

Yeah. Thank you Mr. Chair. And it's always a pleasure to see you. Good people. I apologize for my wonderful fatigues. I'm in hospital. Um, the question I have for the officials is how do you square that? The industry itself is quite critical of the new facility. Uh, and just to extrapolate out some of the questions that have been asked by Mr.

Abel, um, why couldn't a lot of this be privatized and, and reduce the cost? Now you say the risk parameters and or profile are very high, should the this, these biologics be moved, uh, in absence of coming straight to your facility, but how do you square Mr.

Abel's question, which I think is a very salient one direct pathway straight into Christchurch Airport and straight into a private facility that might arguably offer the same services, um, and have a subsidized cost because the industry's saying this is gonna add significant levy costs to them and they can't absorb them.

Uh, Sorry, Mr. Chair, is that a question that should actually go to officials? These aren't, These aren't, um, Well, sure, but the, the, in this, this is hot nz, is it not? Yep. Yep. Well, they might have thoughts on that. So just to clarify, so the question was how do we square, um, some of our own industry having views on this?

Yes. Yes. So, sure. Uh, you have heard last week from some of the importers of Ger Plaza material Yep. How our members are the growers who are then part of that supply chain. They're the end users of the material. Mm. And so they bear the biosecurity risk if, uh, the import system doesn't adequately manage to biosecurity the biosecurity, uh, challenges.

So our membership is very hot on good biosecurity systems. Uh, we did a recent, um, grower sentiment study, uh, survey, and the early indications are that biosecurity was their, their number one, um, concern. So there's obviously gonna be a wide range of views within 4,000 plus members.

Yes, of course. But biosecurity is certainly of concern to our membership. Just to turn, in addition to, to, to to, to extrapolate out Steve Abel's question, do you think there's an avenue for the privatization or the, the co-sharing as it were between, uh, the crown's role as the biosecurity, uh, regulator as it were, and then a private enterprise that could be working in conjunction with them?

Could you see that working? Yeah, I, I think the most important thing is that we need a facility quite quickly. Our current facility is at the end of its life. Um, and we are not aware of anyone else who's putting this on the table. Um, and so we are very supportive of this facility being built.

Okay. Thank you. You right there, mark. Yeah. Thanks Dean. Um, a couple of sub, the submitters that we had last week talked about, um, in the case of overseas jurisdictions where they had, uh, inspection facilities prior to the, uh, in the country of origin before, um, the, uh, ger plasma arrived in their country and it significantly sped up the process.

Do you have any view on that? Because it'd be interesting to hear what the industry thinks. Yeah, so, um, one of our points was that we support the end-to-end review of the PLM import system. And if there are, uh, ways that we can speed the process or reduce the costs, or ideally both, um, whilst adequately managing the biosecurity risks, we would be supportive of that.

So we, uh, we encourage NPI to look broad, um, across the whole system and where those efficiencies can be gained, um, whilst still managing biosecurity to a high level. Thanks very much. Um, look, we've come to time, um, goes very quickly and we appreciate, um, uh, your, uh, submission today.

Mr. Can you provide us your notes here? I didn't get that document. Yes. Um, you can provide it. Yep. Yes. Yep. Brilliant. So we'll move on now to, uh, NPI. Welcome Stuart. Sure. So obviously you've been, um, taking note of the, um, submissions that we had last week plus, um, what happened today.

So, uh, 15 minutes and, and, uh, yeah. I'm sure there'll be plenty of questions too, Stuart. Yeah. Yeah. By away. Thank you. Um, look, thanks, thanks for the opportunity to come this morning. Um, this introduced Lisa Winthrop, uh, she looks at for import health standard part of the whole system. Uh, and Fran is, uh, the director of, uh, our diagnostics, uh, surveillance readiness.

So the laboratory that's being built, um, uh, that whole function. Um, look, I'll just make a few remarks, uh, and then of course, questions and discussion. Um, so look, to start with, I mean, as, as we all know, obviously, um, uh, in Zealand, our food and fiber sector is a huge part of our economy. Over 80% of our, of our export earnings, nearly $60 billion last year, horticultures around $9 billion of that.

Um, we are really fortunate, as we know in New Zealand that we don't have a lot of the pests and disease that other countries struggle with. Um, that gives our growers easier market access. It makes it least costly as well in terms of production. So maintaining that obviously is critical, and that requires a strong biosecurity system. Um, overseas we've seen what can be at stake, uh, the X pathogen in Europe, uh, which has caused billions of euros of damage.

It's decimated olives, um, olives, grapevines, some fruit citrus. Uh, here we particularly worry about things like fruit flies, stink bug, viruses, rusts, uh, that pose major risks to our, to our horticulture sector and to having them here would be devastating. So what we need to do is we need to get that balance right.

Uh, we need, we need to protect our primary industries, our environment. We also, of course, need to allow trade and movement of goods and importation of material. Um, but given what's at stake, um, our biosecurity system, uh, does have some strictest or strongest rules in the world. Um, and that's for good reason because of what, what we need to protect.

So the Plant Health Environment capability program, the laboratory, um, as you know, it's a major investment. It's building this facility in Auckland, uh, a modern high containment diagnostic quarantine facility. Um, it'll future proof our bio security system. Uh, we expect it to last for at least the next 50 years to service us.

Um, and we know that the current facility in Tamaki, it's really end of life. It's too, it's old, it's too small, it can't meet the demands now and going forward, um, important to note, and the comment has been made that, you know, the, the large part of this investment is in the diagnostic part of, of this facility.

Um, so that's, um, that's the diagnostic testing, uh, and all the capability that goes into supporting surveillance by security responses, testing and diagnostics for trade and market assurance. The quarantine facility, uh, for importing plant material is about 10% of the overall cost of that investment.

Um, this facility also will be interoperable for animal diseases. So, uh, if we have a major, major issue, and hopefully we don't, but like foot and mouth disease, bird flu, this facility will also be able to handle animal diseases. So it provides resilience to the system in addition to the Wallace floor facility that we have for animal diseases.

The recent responses around hornets fruit fly, the two fruit flies in Auckland, demonstrates the value of that facility. Um, our scientists and that facility, the current old one, were really at the core of those responses and the diagnostics and the surveillance and supporting that response. So where we're at with it, um, ministers have approved the final business case construction's underway.

We expect it to be open in early, early 2029 for business. The post entry quarantine part of it, for the plant imports, as I say, you know, it's about 10% of the total cost. Um, it does allow plants to be imported, uh, for the horticulture industry to grow the industry. Um, and, and we do know that we need to, we need to quarantine those plants coming in because, um, because of what we're protecting and the risk inherent with that.

Just this year, we have found, um, plant viruses and grape farm stock that we're in the quarantine facility. So we know, um, that it serves a valuable purpose. Um, look, as you know, we are the only facility in the country. Um, we know these facilities are expensive. Um, they provide a very high level of containment.

They have very specialist facilities. Um, and that's one of the reasons why the government's only providing it, acknowledging the price and the cost pressure on the industry for using it. Um, the crown does subsidize the use of, of the quarantine facility. And currently, uh, the crown is subsidizing it to the tune of 50%, uh, the cost of using those facilities.

Uh, we do also offer the option of sharing units to bring down cost, and we are exploring, uh, bringing the time down for certain, certain species and plants, um, that, that they'll spend less time in quarantine. Uh, we do recognize offshore facilities. We've got six that we recognize and allow. Um, but again, um, they do need to meet our standards and, and we have some strict requirements.

So we are open to recognizing more offshore facilities. Just lastly, on the import health standards, which set the rules for how you import things and treatments and what can and can't come in, um, the plant to import system that's been talked about. Look, we know, we know that that system has challenges. We know it takes far too long to produce new standards to update them.

Uh, and it's, and it's a, it's, it's, it's a difficult system to navigate. So hence we are doing that reform and review, and we do want to make it work smarter and, and faster and more efficiently. Um, and what, you know, we're looking at things like ai, uh, ways we can, we can drive efficiencies, ways, we can do it smarter and working with, uh, a number of, of, of stakeholders across industry on that.

So look, thank you for the opportunity and of course, uh, welcome questions. We'll start with Joe. Thank you. Um, thanks for your time with us this morning. Um, I just have a couple of, uh, I guess a couple of questions for you. Um, this new facility in, uh, that's being built, I would, um, suggest perhaps that rather than being an expense, it's actually an investment in our, I would say, primary industries, but also other, other parts of New Zealand. Would you agree with that?

Oh, it is. Look, this is a, this is a huge investment, uh, in biosecurity for the language of New Zealand. Um, it, it, it will provide that critical service. Yeah. Uh, like I said, it's interoperable for animal diseases. Uh, it also has a high quarantine facility that could be used for, for some, uh, human pandemic type, uh, uh, challenges or issues as well.

So yes, it, it's a critical investment in the country's infrastructure. Yeah, that's right. I, yeah, I think it's, um, sometimes we tend to look at things a bit as an expense rather than actually actually an investment, which is, which is what it is. Um, just two other quick questions with regard to the, um, the, the program that you're just undertaking to look at, uh, reducing, um, the length of time this process takes that you mentioned around the, uh, efficiency of the IHE system.

You're doing some trials, you said, with ai, et cetera. Yeah. How long do you expect that sort of program or, um, that to give you an idea of just how more efficient you can become? And then my last question is with regards to other facilities as, um, Steve mentioned maybe at Lincoln or what have you, would that not potentially pose an increased risk given that obviously almost majority of it imports, et cetera, and all that sort of stuff comes into Auckland and then you have to potentially, if this was a thing, transfer it down to transport it to say Christchurch, would

that not increase biosecurity risk by doing that? Are we not better to just keep it at that one new facility that that will be built in specifically for purpose? Yeah. Yeah. Look, thanks. Um, so on the import health standard reforming and getting that working better, so works underway on that.

We've had a number of workshops with, uh, a large number of, of players from across the sector. Um, the ai, uh, we've done a proof of concept. We're now moving into actually, um, building some AI tools that we can use in parts of that process. Uh, we, we, and we've also, we've also worked through some old standards, um, and, uh, cleared some of the amount that, that weren't being used anymore, focus on the priority ones.

We expect, uh, in the next, uh, 12, 12 to 18 months, uh, we will start to see, uh, real changes starting to come in the system and, and things starting to move faster, standards getting updated, produced quicker. Uh, and it will be an ongoing piece of work. There's some, there's, there's some immediate things to get the current process working efficiently, but longer term, there's probably some more fundamental changes to the way the whole thing works as well.

So we, we are open to, and wanting to work through both on the question of the facility and where it is and types of facilities look to start with. We are completely open to and welcoming of anyone else wanting to establish and operate a quarantine facility in New Zealand. Mm-hmm. Um, uh, there is, there, there are, there are no barriers to that.

Um, and we would be supportive, uh, and open to someone else running a quarantine facility. They need to meet the standards and the requirements that go with that, um, for, for our requirements mm-hmm. And, and our trading partners. Mm-hmm. That has been the barrier. These are very, very expensive specialized facilities. Um, and it's really the containment, um, part of it.

Uh, so, you know, a laboratory, uh, is not that expensive to build. It's the containment, uh, to, to contain and stop, you know, micro practical size funding and things like that, that getting out that, that, that, that is the challenge. Um, and then in terms of the location, um, most of our plant material imports, uh, and most of, most of the things that we get over the border in terms of things that need to go to a laboratory to be tested, uh, are in that Auckland region because of the volume of cargo passengers material comes through Auckland.

So, so that's the reason the current facility has been in Auckland for many years. Um, so, so we, so, you know, we worked through quite a process around building any place where would you put it? Is Auckland the best place or not? Um, but we've landed back with Auckland. Auckland is a logical place, and we've got an animal laboratory here in Wallace full up the road.

Um, so having, having two facilities that are interoperable in different parts of the country provides some good resilience for biosecurity as well. We do also have, uh, a small laboratory in Christchurch, um, with flus people. They don't do quarantine and they don't do the high level containment stuff, but the, um, uh, staff and scientists in laboratory there that do diagnostics on PEs and diseases and things that come over the border or that people report.

Thank you, Steve. Yeah. So it's just to be clear, I I don't think anyone's questioning the huge importance of biosecurity. Like it's not contested. Yeah. Um, it's interesting that 10% of the cost of this one is the quarantine, which seems to be the hardest bit of it. And, and 90% of it is the testing and diagnos diagnostics.

Is that correct? And because, because what I'm trying to understand is that, um, if the capability of the character of the technology that can do that diagnostics has become a lot more available and lower cost in recent years, would we be spending the money the same way now if we had that same amount of money mm-hmm.

In terms of investment in overall biosecurity, is this place that we would be spending it, and is that where there's an opportunity for greater efficiencies and greater cost saving without any compromise of biosecurity? I, I'll, well let flu answer that question.

Yeah. So just, um, to pick up on what you said, so approximately 10% of the cost associated with the post entry quarantine greenhouse, which is a former of Biocon containment, the other 90% is associated with building the biocon containment laboratories. So that's, um, physical containment level two and three for microorganisms invertebrates.

Right. Um, and plants, um, the biocon containment for microorganisms at level three is extremely expensive, right. 'cause of the airflow, the pressure. Yep. Uh, the hip filtration, the need to test, um, to treat all of the wastewater. Um, and that's what is the biggest driver of the cost to the facility.

Gotcha. Um, but speaking, I guess to your questions around diag diagnostic technology. Mm. Um, diagnostic technology is constantly evolving and my team is, uh, constantly developing new tests. So we, uh, the first laboratory in New Zealand that's been accredited to do, um, high throughput sequencing, um, for a range of, um, diseases, uh, we actually can do more high throughput sequence testing, um, of commodities than, um, Australia does on their facility.

Uh, we've also put in place, um, EDNA, um, testing for monitoring and testing of a number of viruses. We are looking into it for surveillance, um, of mid vectors of arboviruses. Uh, and we've also, um, recently implemented DNA based, um, surveillance for B mites.

Mm-hmm. Now, the challenge for us in our current facility is we are constrained in deploying new technologies because the biocon containment is just not keeping up with modern standards. And also in order to work efficiently and avoid the risk of cross contamination, when you're doing this mm, you need the additional space.

Um, I quite often get people say to me, why are you building such a big lab? You know, you're gonna fill it with a whole lot of people. And my answer is actually no. Um, because in the future we are gonna be deploying more automation and more robotics, and guess what? Those machines take a lot more space. Um, and we've seen that happen at Willville.

We've, we've deployed, um, robotics for FMD testing that as soon as you put those machines in the lab, there's not a lot of room for the people. So we are very, very mindful of, um, needing to design this in a way that we can flex and deploy new technologies. The Final one, Mr. Chair. Yep. Quickly, um, do you support this in end-to-end review of the current import system that H New Zealand called for?

Yes. Yeah. And that's, and that's what we're working on, uh, starting off with plans, uh, in terms of, so the import health standard, it's really the import health standard process in terms of you want to import something into the country, um, what is that end to end process to do it, uh, import health standards is a big part in that, but they feed into the quarantine requirements and treatments and so on.

So yes, that is, that is the work and the program that we have underway, uh, across that end to end, we are the efficiencies, uh, that we can make some fundamental performance. Mm-hmm. Yeah. Alright. We've got time for One last question from Mark Cameron. Make a quick mark. We're running outta Time. Thanks. Very quickly, Stuart, how do you square away the criticism from the industry or someone in the industry that has said that it's an exorbitant cost and it's gonna add significant levies that will only stifle innovation

there in, how do you square that? Yeah, look, uh, no thanks, mark. Um, and, and look, as I said, you know, we are sympathetic to the costs on users of this facility, uh, for quarantine. Um, uh, you know, the, the, the, it's, it's, it's tricky. You know, there, there's the balance of obviously what we're trying to protect the cost of these facilities to actually have them.

Hence there aren't private sector operators of them. Uh, so what, you know, what, what we can do best there, mark, is the things we've, we've talked about, um, to start with currently as things stand without changing anything, you know, we are subsidizing 50% of the cost to the users. Mm-hmm. Um, then the things like bringing the time down, um, if we can, if, if we are able to, to verify and certify more offshore facilities.

Absolutely. And then these changes to the system whereby, um, uh, you know, some of the requirements, uh, could be amended that either again, make it quicker, shorter, easier to get through, um, or with new, new, new diagnostics and testing. So there's a suite of things in there. There isn't a silver bullet.

Um, but we are committed to working through, uh, and with industry and with all the stakeholders to actually just get this working the most efficient way it can, balancing why we need it and what we're trying to protect as well. Thanks, Stuart. Very grateful. Thanks very much. Um, we've come to time, so I appreciate, um, you taking the time to come and see us.
We may have a couple of written questions perhaps. Sure, Absolutely. Yeah. Yeah. But thank you very much. Okay.
